First identified barrier to DOT enterprise adoption

notes: No clear ROI case established for DOT in 1997, per Aberdeen

notes: CORBA/DCOM were superseded by SOAP web services then REST APIs by mid-2000s; distributed computing did win but via different standards (source: ACM Queue 'Rise and Fall of CORBA' 2006)

metric_value: CORBA implied as viable standard path
notes: Report discusses CORBA barriers but implies it will mature into a viable standard

metric_value: CORBA declined to niche status by mid-2000s
notes: CORBA became niche technology by mid-2000s due to complexity and vendor fragmentation; Microsoft dropped DCOM; both replaced by web services (Wikipedia CORBA article; ACM Queue 2006)

metric_value: Internet will be key deployment target for DOT
notes: Report specifically calls out Internet competitive-advantage applications as prime DOT use case

metric_name: Internet as distributed computing driver - outcome
notes: Internet did become the dominant distributed computing environment; however via HTTP/REST not CORBA/DCOM
